abstracts.title: Insurance Requirements
abstracts.desc: Association master policy and unit owner coverage obligations under the Declaration.
abstracts.subtitle: The Association maintains comprehensive insurance for common areas and facilities, while unit owners must cover personal property and unit-specific risks.

blocks.heading: Association Insurance Policies
blocks.text: The Association is obligated to maintain comprehensive coverage for common areas and facilities, funded through owner assessments based on ownership percentages.

cards.label: Fire Insurance
cards.text: Full insurable replacement value of common areas and facilities, without deduction for depreciation.

cards.label: Liability Insurance
cards.text: $200,000 per person / $500,000 per accident / $50,000 property damage — subject to annual review and Board increase.

cards.label: Workers' Comp
cards.text: Required under Washington law for employees or contractors on Association property.

cards.label: Fidelity Bonds
cards.text: Required for Board members and relevant personnel to protect against financial misconduct.

sourceText.label: View exact policy language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 12.1
passages: The Association must secure fire insurance with extended coverage endorsement, covering the full insurable replacement value of the common areas and facilities, without deduction for depreciation.

Liability insurance with minimum coverage limits of $200,000 per person injured, $500,000 per accident, and $50,000 for property damage. These limits are subject to annual review and may be increased at the Board’s discretion.
Workers’ compensation insurance as required by applicable Washington state laws.
Fidelity bonds for Board members and other relevant personnel to protect against financial misconduct.

blocks.heading: Your Insurance Obligations
blocks.text: You are responsible for insuring everything the Association’s policy doesn’t cover — your personal property, unit-specific risks, and any improvements over $1,000.

cards.label: Personal Property
cards.text: Insurance for all personal belongings within your unit — the Association’s policies do not cover individual items.

cards.label: Improvements Over $1K
cards.text: You must notify the Board of any improvements valued over $1,000 and file copies of insurance policies covering them.

cards.label: Unit-Specific Risks
cards.text: Coverage for damages caused by plumbing, appliances, or equipment within your unit — including hot water tank leaks.

sourceText.label: View exact language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 12
passages: Owners must secure insurance for personal property within their units, as the Association’s policies do not cover individual belongings.
Owners are required to notify the Board of any improvements to their unit valued over $1,000, as these may affect the overall insurable value of the condominium.
Owners must file copies of any individual insurance policies covering such improvements with the Association.
Owners are responsible for insuring against risks specific to their units, such as damages caused by plumbing fixtures, appliances, or other equipment.

action.label: If damage occurs in your unit
action.title: What to do when you need to file a claim
steps: Document the damage — take photos and identify the source.
Determine responsibility — if damage is from your unit’s plumbing or appliances, your policy applies. If it involves common areas, contact management.
Report to AGM Real Estate Group — call (206) 622-8600 for any damage affecting common areas or other units.
File with your insurer — you are responsible for unit-specific damage regardless of Association coverage.

blocks.heading: Special Conditions
items: The Association’s insurer’s liability is not affected by other insurance obtained by unit owners — no set-off, counterclaim, or contribution.
Policies must include a mortgagee clause protecting institutional first mortgagees. Settlements exceeding $2,500 require mortgagee approval.
Insurance policies must not be affected by hazardous use or vacancy of the property.
In cases of damage, the Board may authorize emergency work funded by insurance proceeds or assessments.
In the event of condemnation, insurance proceeds are apportioned among owners based on their undivided interest in common areas, with the Board allocating awards.

sourceText.label: View exact provisions
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Articles 12–13
passages: The Association’s insurance policies must include provisions ensuring that the insurer’s liability is not affected by other insurance obtained by unit owners. This prevents set-off, counterclaim, apportionment, proration, or contribution by reason of additional owner policies.

Policies must include a mortgagee clause that protects institutional first mortgagees. Any settlement exceeding $2,500 requires mortgagee approval, provided such approval is not unreasonably withheld.
Insurance policies must not be affected by hazardous use or vacancy of the property, ensuring continuous coverage.
In cases of damage or destruction, the Board may authorize emergency work to prevent further damage, funded by insurance proceeds or assessments (Article 13.3.2).

blocks.heading: Board Oversight & Owner Obligations
items: The Board annually reviews and adjusts insurance coverage limits, obtaining at least two firm bids for repair estimates.
Owners must notify the Board of improvements exceeding $1,000 and provide copies of individual insurance policies.
Insurance costs are included in common expense assessments based on ownership percentage.
Non-payment of assessments can lead to liens or foreclosure.

takeaways: The Association maintains fire, liability, workers’ comp, and fidelity bond coverage for common areas — funded through your assessments.
You must insure your personal property, unit-specific risks, and any improvements over $1,000.
Notify the Board and file insurance copies for any unit improvements valued over $1,000.
Mortgagee approval is required for insurance settlements exceeding $2,500.
Non-payment of assessments (which fund insurance) can lead to liens or foreclosure.

abstracts.title: Maintenance Responsibilities
abstracts.desc: Unit owner and Association upkeep obligations, emergency procedures, and enforcement.
abstracts.subtitle: Rules for property maintenance are outlined in the Declaration, Bylaws, and House Rules to balance individual owner obligations with collective responsibilities.

blocks.heading: What You Maintain
blocks.text: You are responsible for your unit interior, assigned limited common areas (decks, lanais), and ensuring your tenants comply with all rules.
items: Interior maintenance — appliances, plumbing, electrical fixtures, wiring, water heaters, wall and floor coverings, and mailboxes.
Limited common areas — decks and lanais must be maintained without modification or damage (no drilling, no heavy planters directly on surfaces).
Tenant oversight — provide tenants with House Rules and ensure compliance. You are liable for tenant-caused damage.
Leak prevention — turn off washer valves when away and maintain hot water tanks.
Board approval required for any structural or exterior changes.
Move-in/move-out — coordinate activities, pay associated fees ($250), and remove trash promptly.
Report damage like cuts or cracks in deck coatings to the management company promptly.

sourceText.label: View exact language from House Rules
sourceText.heading: Declaration & House Rules
passages: Owners are responsible for maintaining, repairing, and replacing all elements within their unit, including appliances, plumbing, electrical fixtures, wiring, water heaters, wall and floor coverings, and mailboxes.
Owners must maintain assigned limited common areas (e.g., decks, lanais) without modifying or damaging them. No drilling into exterior stucco. No heavy planters directly on deck surfaces.
Owners leasing units must ensure tenants comply with all rules, provide tenants with House Rules, and are liable for tenant-caused damage.
Owners must prevent leaks (e.g., turn off washer valves when away, maintain hot water tanks) and avoid actions increasing insurance rates.

action.label: If something needs repair
action.title: How to handle a maintenance issue
steps: Determine responsibility — interior components and unit-exclusive systems are yours. Common areas and building systems are the Association’s.
If it’s yours — arrange repairs directly. Get Board approval for any exterior or structural work.
If it’s the Association’s — report to AGM Real Estate Group at (206) 622-8600.
If it’s urgent — report immediately. You may be liable for damage caused by delay.

blocks.heading: What the Association Maintains
blocks.text: The HOA handles all common areas, building-wide systems, and exterior building components.

cards.label: Common Areas
cards.text: Hallways, stairwells, lobbies, garages, grounds, roofs.

cards.label: Exterior Components
cards.text: Railings, exterior deck surfaces, and building facade.

cards.label: Building Systems
cards.text: Water, sewer, and electrical services affecting the entire building.

cards.label: Administration
cards.text: Reserve accounts, budgets, assessments, and contractor management.

blocks.heading: Emergency Repairs
blocks.text: The HOA can authorize immediate repairs for emergencies, with costs covered by reserve funds or special assessments. The Board may confer via phone or email to expedite decisions.

pullQuote.text: Report urgent issues to AGM Real Estate Group at (206) 622-8600 for prompt action. For significant emergencies, the Board may confer via phone or email to expedite decisions.
pullQuote.caption: Emergency contact and procedures

blocks.heading: How Maintenance Is Funded
blocks.text: Monthly assessments cover routine maintenance. A reserve account handles unexpected costs. Special assessments may apply for major projects.
items: Monthly assessments based on each owner’s percentage of ownership in common areas cover water, sewer, garbage, and common area upkeep.
Reserve account maintained for unforeseen or unbudgeted costs.
Special assessments for specific repairs or major projects exceeding $5,000 may require owner approval.
Move-in/move-out fee: $250. Reserved parking: $44–$110.

blocks.heading: What Happens If You Don’t Comply
blocks.text: The enforcement structure is clear and escalating — from warnings to fines to liens to legal action.
items: First infraction: written warning.
Second infraction: minimum $50 fine.
Continuing infractions: minimum $200 fine until corrected.
Tenant violations: $100 per occurrence.
Unapproved rentals: $200 fine.
Non-payment within 30 days leads to a lien on the unit.
Deck damage from negligence could exceed $8,000 in repair costs.
The HOA may pursue legal action with attorney fees charged to the owner.
Unauthorized parking in non-deeded spaces risks vehicle towing at the owner’s expense.
Non-compliance with rental rules may result in loss of rental privileges or termination of parking agreements.

sourceText.label: View exact fine schedule
sourceText.heading: House Rules — Addendum 4
passages: Violations incur a warning for the first infraction, a minimum $50 fine for the second, and a minimum $200 fine for continuing infractions until corrected.
Owners are liable for repair costs due to negligence or unauthorized changes, e.g., deck damage from improper planter placement or glued carpet.
Unpaid fines or repair costs result in a lien against the owner’s unit.
Owners are responsible for attorney fees and costs if legal action is needed to enforce compliance.
sourceText.ref: Shannon Heights House Rules, Addendum 4; Declaration, Sections 11.2 & 18.1

takeaways: You maintain your unit interior and assigned decks/lanais. The HOA handles common areas and building systems.
Report emergencies to AGM Real Estate Group at (206) 622-8600 immediately.
Monthly assessments fund routine maintenance. Reserve funds cover unexpected costs.
Fines escalate from warnings to $50 to $200+ for continuing violations. Deck negligence alone can cost over $8,000.
You are liable for damage caused by you, your tenants, or your guests.
Contact AGM Real Estate Group (206-622-8600) for all maintenance requests.

abstracts.title: ACC Requests
abstracts.desc: Architectural Control Committee approval process for modifications to units and common areas.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors manages the ACC function, ensuring a uniform exterior appearance and structural integrity at Shannon Heights.

blocks.heading: What Requires Approval?
blocks.text: All modifications to exteriors, structures, or common/limited common areas require prior written Board approval. When in doubt, ask first.
items: Exterior modifications — painting, altering doors, windows, decks, or any exterior-visible portion of a unit.
Structural changes — including subdividing or combining units (requires 66⅔% owner vote).
Common and limited common areas — attaching plant boxes, installing carpet, adding trellises to hallways, stairwells, or roof decks.
Signs — no signs, notices, or advertisements except approved “For Sale” signs.

sourceText.label: View exact requirements from governing documents
sourceText.heading: Declaration & House Rules
passages: All alterations or modifications to a Unit—including changes to flooring, plumbing, electrical systems, exterior elements (such as windows), or any modifications impacting Common or Limited Common Elements—require prior written approval from the Board of Directors.

Any painting, modification, or alteration to the building, decks, doors, windows, or other exterior-visible portions of a unit requires Board approval (House Rules, Rule 17; Declaration, Section 10.5).
Alterations to structural components of a unit, including subdividing or combining units, require a vote of owners holding more than 66⅔% of the voting power (Declaration, Section 24.1.2).

Changes to common areas (hallways, stairwells, roof decks) or limited common areas (unit decks) require approval — including plant boxes, carpet, and trellises (House Rules, Rule 16; Declaration, Section 10.11).
No signs, notices, or advertisements may be displayed except approved ‘For Sale’ signs (House Rules, Rule 5, Addendum 2).
sourceText.ref: Shannon Heights Declaration, Sections 10.5, 10.11, 24.1; House Rules, Rules 5, 16, 17

action.label: Before you start any modification
action.title: ACC approval step by step
steps: Contact AGM Real Estate Group — call (206) 622-8600 to submit your written request with detailed plans and specifications.
Board reviews for compliance — the Board evaluates against governing documents.
Structural changes need 66⅔% vote — subdividing or combining units requires owner supermajority approval.
Obtain permits and insurance — you may need Lake Forest Park city building permits and additional insurance coverage.
Board responds within 10 days — for rental-related requests; similar timelines apply for ACC requests.

blocks.heading: Exterior & Deck Rules
blocks.text: The Board strictly controls exterior uniformity. Deck maintenance rules are especially detailed given the building’s construction.
items: Modifications must not deviate from the original building appearance — deck coatings and colors controlled by the Board.
No screws, nails, or adhesives may penetrate deck surfaces.
Heavy planters must be supported on foam or neoprene and precast concrete pavers.
Outdoor carpeting requires Board approval — must be loose-laid, non-absorbent, and removed seasonally.
No drilling into exterior stucco. No storing unsightly items on decks.
No lattice or trellises attached to deck railings. No hot grease or caustic materials on decks.
All changes must comply with King County and Lake Forest Park regulations.

sourceText.label: View full deck rules from House Rules
sourceText.heading: House Rules — Rule 16
passages: No screws, nails, or adhesives may penetrate deck surfaces. Heavy planters must be supported on foam or neoprene and precast concrete pavers.
Outdoor carpeting requires Board approval and must be loose-laid, non-absorbent, and removed seasonally.
No drilling into exterior stucco, storing unsightly items, or attaching lattice/trellises to deck railings.
Hot grease or caustic/abrasive materials are banned on decks.
Deck coatings and colors are controlled by the Board. All changes must adhere to King County and Lake Forest Park regulations.

blocks.heading: Non-Compliance
blocks.text: The fine structure escalates, and deck damage from negligence can be extremely costly.
items: First infraction: written warning.
Second infraction: minimum $50 fine.
Continuing infractions: minimum $200 fine until corrected.
Owners liable for all repair costs from negligence or unauthorized changes.
Unpaid fines or repair costs result in a lien against the unit.
Legal action costs — attorney fees charged to the owner.

pullQuote.text: Deck damage from negligence (improper planters, glued carpet, drilling) could exceed $8,000 in repair costs — all charged to the owner.
pullQuote.caption: Deck damage liability

takeaways: All exterior, structural, and common area changes require prior written Board approval.
Structural changes like combining units need 66⅔% owner vote.
Deck rules are strict — no penetrations, no adhesives, seasonal carpet only. Negligence can cost $8,000+.
Fines escalate from warning to $50 to $200+ for ongoing violations.
Contact AGM Real Estate Group (206-622-8600) to submit ACC requests.

abstracts.title: Board of Directors
abstracts.desc: Governance structure, elections, authority, and current board composition.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors manages condominium operations, enforces rules, oversees finances, and maintains common areas. Directors serve without compensation and prioritize the interests of all unit owners.

cards.label: Legal Status
cards.text: Shannon Heights Owners Association is a nonprofit corporation under Washington law.

cards.label: Board Size
cards.text: Five (5) members after Declarant control ends.

cards.label: Officers
cards.text: President, Vice-President, Secretary, and Treasurer — elected annually by the Board.

cards.label: Hearing Board
cards.text: Established to investigate, hear, and determine complaints about rule violations with authority to impose fines.

blocks.heading: How Elections Work
blocks.text: Directors are elected at annual meetings. The initial Board serves a one-year term; subsequent terms are governed by the Bylaws.
items: Vacancies (other than removals) are filled by a majority vote of the remaining Board members, serving until the next annual meeting.
Initial Board elected at first annual meeting after Declarant control ends.

pullQuote.text: Any Board member may be removed, with or without cause, by a majority vote of all apartment owners at a regular or special meeting. The member proposed for removal is given an opportunity to be heard.
pullQuote.caption: Director removal — Bylaws, Article 3.2.3

sourceText.label: View exact removal language
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Article 3.2.3
passages: Any Board member may be removed, with or without cause, by a majority vote of all apartment owners at a regular or special meeting called for that purpose.
The Board member proposed for removal is given an opportunity to be heard at the meeting.
Until the Declarant’s management authority ends, only the Declarant may remove Board members.
Removal requires an affirmative vote of a majority of all unit owners, not just those present.

blocks.heading: Meetings & Decision-Making
items: At least two regular meetings per fiscal year, plus one immediately after the annual owners’ meeting.
Special meetings called by the President or two Board members with 3 days’ notice.
Majority of Board members constitutes a quorum; majority vote of those present is binding.
Notice may be waived in writing or by attendance.

sourceText.label: View meeting rules
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Articles 3.2.5–3.2.8
passages: Regular meetings of the Board are held at least twice per fiscal year, including one immediately following the annual owners’ meeting, with at least three days’ notice to Board members.
Special meetings may be called by the President or upon written request of at least two Board members, with three days’ notice specifying time, place, and purpose.
A majority of Board members constitutes a quorum, and decisions require a majority vote of those present at a meeting with a quorum.
Notice may be waived in writing or by attendance, and no notice is required if all Board members are present.

blocks.heading: Officers of the Board
blocks.text: Officers are elected annually by the Board at the organization meeting. Officers may be removed by majority Board vote. Officers may be removed by a majority vote of the Board, with or without cause, at any regular or special meeting.

cards.label: President
cards.text: Chief executive — presides over meetings, appoints committees.

cards.label: Vice-President
cards.text: Assumes President’s duties in their absence.

cards.label: Secretary
cards.text: Keeps minutes, maintains records and member lists with mailing addresses.

cards.label: Treasurer
cards.text: Oversees financial records, assessments, and deposits.

cards.label: Assistants
cards.text: Assistant Secretaries and Treasurers may be appointed to serve in the absence of primary officers.

blocks.heading: Compensation & Liability
blocks.text: Directors serve without compensation. The Association indemnifies Board members for actions taken in good faith, except for willful misconduct. Indemnification does not extend to liabilities incurred as apartment owners.

blocks.heading: What the Board Can Do
blocks.text: The Board has broad authority to manage the Association, enforce rules, and pursue legal remedies.
items: Enforce the Declaration, Bylaws, and House Rules.
Manage common areas including maintenance, repairs, and structural alterations.
Administer assessments, reserve accounts, and financial matters.
Oversee insurance coverage — fire, liability, and fidelity bonds.
Hire management companies and legal/accounting services.
Adopt, amend, or revoke administrative rules.
Enter apartments for emergency repairs or maintenance.
Collect rent from tenants to cover delinquent assessments.
Pursue legal action for non-compliance with attorney fees charged to the owner.
Enter apartments for maintenance, repairs, or emergencies, with authority to access units with minimal inconvenience.
Hire legal and accounting services as needed, with authority to pursue legal remedies for non-compliance.

sourceText.label: View full authority from governing documents
sourceText.heading: Bylaws & Declaration — Board Authority
passages: Enforce the Declaration, Bylaws, and House Rules, including compliance with the Washington Horizontal Property Regimes Act (Article 1.1, Article 6.1).
Manage common areas, including maintenance, repairs, and structural alterations, with authority to access apartments for these purposes (Article 6.3).
Administer financial matters, including assessments, reserve accounts, and unforeseen expenses (Article 6.1; Declaration Article 11).
Oversee insurance coverage, including fire, liability, and fidelity bonds for officers handling funds (Article 3.2.9).
Handle legal and accounting services as needed, with authority to pursue legal remedies for non-compliance (Article 14.3.5).
Adopt, amend, or revoke administrative rules and regulations to supplement the Declaration and Bylaws (Article 6.5.9).

The Board has authority to enforce covenants, conditions, and restrictions, including imposing fines (up to a maximum set by Board resolution) and pursuing legal action (Article 14.4; Article 14.3.5).
The Board may enter apartments for emergency repairs or maintenance with minimal inconvenience, with immediate entry permitted in emergencies (Article 6.3).
The Board may collect rent from tenants to cover delinquent assessments (Declaration Article 11.14).
Non-compliance by owners does not waive future enforcement.

blocks.heading: Hearing Board
blocks.text: A formal Hearing Board investigates and determines complaints about rule violations. Orders are effective 10 days after service unless otherwise specified.

sourceText.label: View Hearing Board provisions
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Articles 14.2–14.4
passages: A Hearing Board is established to investigate, hear, and determine complaints regarding violations of Governing Documents, with authority to impose fines.
The Hearing Board handles complaints, conducts hearings, and issues decisions on violations, with orders effective 10 days after service unless otherwise specified.
The Board may impose fines up to a maximum set by Board resolution and pursue legal remedies for non-compliance.

pullQuote.text: Amendments to the Bylaws require approval by owners holding 66⅔% of the voting power at a regular or special meeting, with at least 10 days’ notice.
pullQuote.caption: Amendment threshold — Bylaws, Article 7.1

takeaways: Five Board members govern Shannon Heights with officers elected annually.
Any director can be removed by majority vote of all owners (not just those present).
A formal Hearing Board handles violation complaints with authority to impose fines.
The Board can enter apartments for emergencies and collect rent from tenants for delinquent assessments.
Bylaw amendments require 66⅔% owner approval with 10 days’ notice.
Directors serve without compensation and are indemnified for good-faith actions.

abstracts.title: Resale Certificate
abstracts.desc: Legally mandated disclosures required for the sale or transfer of any unit.
abstracts.subtitle: A Resale Certificate is required for every unit sale at Shannon Heights, providing buyers with critical financial, governance, and operational disclosures.

blocks.heading: Why It Exists
blocks.text: The Resale Certificate ensures transparency for prospective buyers by disclosing the financial status, governance structure, and obligations of the Association. It is mandated by RCW 64.34.425.

blocks.heading: Who Prepares It?
blocks.text: AGM Real Estate Group prepares the certificate at the seller’s request and expense. It must be delivered to the buyer within 10 days.

action.label: If you’re selling your unit
action.title: How to get your Resale Certificate
steps: Contact AGM Real Estate Group — call (206) 622-8600 to request the certificate.
Pay the preparation fee — approximately $200–$300 (subject to change).
Clear all outstanding obligations — assessments, fines, or fees must be paid before transfer.
Deliver to the buyer before closing — the buyer has 7 days to cancel after receiving it.

blocks.heading: Financial Disclosures
items: Current monthly assessment fees and special assessments.
Outstanding fees or fines on the unit.
Reserve account status and budget details.

blocks.heading: Governance & Legal
items: Copies of Declaration, Bylaws, House Rules, and amendments.
Pending or threatened litigation involving the Association.
Known violations of House Rules specific to the unit.

blocks.heading: Property & Insurance
items: Association insurance coverage details — limits and deductibles.
Status of major repairs or capital improvements planned or in progress.
Restrictions on unit use or leasing.
Reserve study information.

pullQuote.text: Buyers have a 7-day right to cancel the purchase agreement after receiving the Resale Certificate. Cancellation must be in writing. Any earnest money is refunded without penalty.
pullQuote.caption: Buyer cancellation right — RCW 64.34.425(3)

blocks.heading: Fees & Seller Responsibility
blocks.text: The seller pays the preparation fee ($200–$300), must clear all assessments and fines before transfer, and may owe a $250 move-in/move-out fee. The Association may place a lien for unpaid amounts.

blocks.heading: Why Compliance Matters
items: Failure to provide a Resale Certificate can delay or void the sale.
Incomplete or inaccurate certificates may lead to buyer cancellation or legal disputes.
The Association may pursue legal action for outstanding fees, with attorney fees charged to the seller.

takeaways: A Resale Certificate is mandatory for every unit sale — required by Washington law.
Contact AGM Real Estate Group (206-622-8600) to request it. Preparation costs $200–$300.
The Association must deliver within 10 days of the request.
Buyers have 7 days to cancel after receiving the certificate (not the usual 5 days at other properties).
Clear all assessments, fines, and fees before closing to avoid liens or delays.
Move-in/move-out fee: $250.

abstracts.title: Quick Reference
abstracts.desc: Key numbers, deadlines, and thresholds from all governing documents in one place.
abstracts.subtitle: Every important number, deadline, and threshold from across all Shannon Heights governing document abstracts.

cards.label: Property Management
cards.text: AGM Real Estate Group — (206) 622-8600

cards.label: ACC Requests
cards.text: Submit written requests through AGM Real Estate Group.

cards.label: Emergency Repairs
cards.text: Call AGM Real Estate Group at (206) 622-8600 immediately.

cards.label: Resale Certificates
cards.text: Request through AGM Real Estate Group — $200–$300 fee.

cards.label: 1st Infraction
cards.text: Written warning.

cards.label: 2nd Infraction
cards.text: Minimum $50 fine.

cards.label: Continuing
cards.text: Minimum $200 fine until corrected.

cards.label: Tenant Violations
cards.text: $100 per occurrence.

cards.label: Unapproved Rental
cards.text: $200 fine.

cards.label: Deck Negligence
cards.text: Repair costs can exceed $8,000.

cards.label: Resale Certificate
cards.text: $200–$300 (seller pays).

cards.label: Improvement Notice
cards.text: Required for unit improvements over $1,000.

cards.label: Remove Director
cards.text: Majority of all owners (not just those present).

cards.label: Amend Bylaws
cards.text: 66⅔% of voting power with 10 days’ notice.

cards.label: Structural Changes
cards.text: 66⅔% of voting power for subdividing/combining units.

cards.label: Board Quorum
cards.text: Majority of Board members.

cards.label: Insurance Settlements
cards.text: Mortgagee approval required for settlements over $2,500.

cards.label: Fire Coverage
cards.text: Full insurable replacement value, no depreciation deduction.

cards.label: Liability Per Person
cards.text: $200,000 minimum.

cards.label: Liability Per Accident
cards.text: $500,000 minimum.

cards.label: Property Damage
cards.text: $50,000 minimum.

cards.label: Buyer Cancellation
cards.text: 7 days after receiving Resale Certificate.

takeaways: AGM Real Estate Group (206-622-8600) handles ACC requests, maintenance, emergencies, and resale certificates.
Fines escalate: warning → $50 → $200+ for continuing violations. Deck negligence can cost $8,000+.
Bylaw amendments and structural changes both require 66⅔% owner approval.
Directors can be removed by majority of all owners — not just those present at the meeting.
Buyers get 7 days to cancel after receiving the Resale Certificate.
Notify the Board of any unit improvements over $1,000 and file insurance copies.
